posts.name: Doug Lee
posts.cooked:
• 2001 Domaine Joseph Roty Charmes-Chambertin Très Vieilles Vignes - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Charmes-Chambertin Grand Cru (11/4/2017)
    Popped and aerated in the glass for 30 minutes. Still a darker ruby color. Fresh nose of crushed black raspberry, plum, dense floral notes, forest floor, stone. Layers of lively but muscular black cherry and raspberry fruit on the palate cut with iodine and hints of caramel which deepened with time to include earth, sweet grip, and brier. About three hours in the palate was singing. Crisp acidity and firm tannin with some mild oak notes. Longer finish of musky dark fruit and rock. The wine showed well but last sips were best, indicating the need for more time in the cellar or a healthy decant. (92 pts.)

Posted from CellarTracker

posts.name: Thomas De Waen
posts.cooked: Had the 1989 version of this not long ago and it felt like it needed 5 more years in bottle! So dense… almost feels like Bordeaux. On the other hand a 1985 1er fontenys of theirs at ma cuisine a couple of years ago was sensational…

posts.name: Doug Lee
posts.cooked:
Thomas,

Yes I agree (based upon an N of 1) these are long lived wines. Two more left in the cellar, which will sleep for many more years.

Cheers,
Doug

posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked: Based upon my limited experience with Roty’s wines, that is a very good note, Doug. If Barthod, Clavelier and the like are at one end of the stylistic spectrum (or in one corner of Keith Levenberg’s stylistic quadrant), Roty is at/in the opposite one. Served to me blind, I am not sure that I would guess Burgundy. The density and carmel/oak are not for me.

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: I always call Roty the Dunn of Burgundy, backwards wines. He was a heavy smoker and his wines are dense and have plenty of oak, probably reflecting his palate sensitivity. He died a few years ago of throat cancer, son now making the wines, and I have not tasted any of the newer wines.

posts.name: Stephen Williams
posts.cooked: I had an ‘85 recently…just about ready. Incredibly good too !

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
alan weinberg:

I always call Roty the Dunn of Burgundy, backwards wines. He was a heavy smoker and his wines are dense and have plenty of oak, probably reflecting his palate sensitivity. He died a few years ago of throat cancer, son now making the wines, and I have not tasted any of the newer wines.

Joseph died in 2008, and sadly his son Philippe followed him (also of cancer) just after the 2015 harvest. Philippe’s brother, Pierre-Jean, is now making the wines.

My understanding is that Philippe had already taken over the reins in the cellar by the mid-1990s, so you will likely have tasted his wines. Oak (and often quite toasty oak) is certainly part of the Domaine style, but to my palate the wines have enough substance, thanks to extremely old vines and low yields, to integrate it. With bottle age, I would rank the domaine’s three grand crus among the best wines produced in Gevrey.

posts.name: Doug Lee
posts.cooked:
Martin, Alan, Stephen, and William,

Thank you for your enlightened comments. This bottle was, for my experience, more characterized by density and oak influence than what I am used to. It was enjoyable ultimately. I hope that William is right regarding more integration of oak over the long haul. What the remaining bottles need is time, and plenty of it.

Cheers,
Doug

posts.name: p@ulbortin
posts.cooked: Nice note that could be used to describe the 2002 we opened recently. Blind, I would have thought Vogue Bonnes Mares. Nothing charming about this Charmes.

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked:
alan weinberg:

I always call Roty the Dunn of Burgundy, backwards wines. He was a heavy smoker and his wines are dense and have plenty of oak, probably reflecting his palate sensitivity. He died a few years ago of throat cancer, son now making the wines, and I have not tasted any of the newer wines.

This is true of most of the line-up, but at the top I’ve found the wines to be very good–93-95-96 Charmes and Mazis have been very good.

posts.name: Jürgen Steinke
posts.cooked:
I agree with William again. IMO Roty produces wines in the class of Rousseau. They just need time.
BTW: For those who are interested in value – the Marsannay line up of Roty is fabulous.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Jürgen Steinke:

I agree with William again. IMO Roty produces wines in the class of Rousseau. They just need time.
BTW: For those who are interested in value – the Marsannay line up of Roty is fabulous.

When you compare like with like, they are arguably better, as Roty’s Mazis and Charmes are a step up over Rousseau’s relatively (and strangely) weak renditions of those two vineyards. Admittedly, Roty’s vines are better-situated in both climats.

posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked:
Jürgen Steinke:

BTW: For those who are interested in value – the Marsannay line up of Roty is fabulous.

They fall right in line with the style described in this thread. I find the density, concentration and oak to be really tough going.

Stephen Williams:

I had an ‘85 recently…just about ready. Incredibly good too !

Me too - though about 2 years ago - and I would agree - almost ready, and it remains the only ‘almost ready’ TVV I’ve ever drunk. On that night, served with the top two (85s) from Rousseau - they were all different, but none was ‘better’. I’m no fan of the oak - but at least after 30 years it was barely discernable. I’m far too old to buy wines that are oaked like this - but the Rousseau Chambertin is also not oaked for the faint-hearted - fortunately their rendition of oak is largely gone after ‘only’ 15 years…

posts.name: J0zef P1nxten
posts.cooked: In Burgundy this weekend, Max Cheurlin at Georges Noellat surprised me by saying that Roty was very well regarded back in the day (70s - 80s), so this goes in the same direction - interesting to see how domaines go in and out of fashion.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
jwpinxten:

In Burgundy this weekend, Max Cheurlin at Georges Noellat surprised me by saying that Roty was very well regarded back in the day (70s - 80s), so this goes in the same direction - interesting to see how domaines go in and out of fashion.

They only show wines from bottle, thus missing out on the hype of vintage reports, and they are also quite “pudique” and discreet as a family, not much given to self-promotion. I think it’s as simple as that.

posts.name: Kevin_S_hin
posts.cooked:
Joseph Roty always has been slightly under the radar but had a decent following.

  • 2002 Domaine Joseph Roty Mazis-Chambertin - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Mazis-Chambertin Grand Cru (5/31/2016)
    TN: Trump BdB, Chardonnay, Piedmont and Burgundy (The Grill Room at Rosewood DC): Very similar to the last bottle had a year ago. Expressive nose displaying pure and fresh black fruits, blackberry, coffee, a hint of licorice, rose and mineral. Exceptional concentration, beautifully layered fruits, decadent pure black fruit driven palate impression, perfect amount of acidity, strong presence of mineral and lovely long finish. Generous opulent black fruit makes the wine really enjoyable. Really nice. (96 pts.)
  • 1996 Domaine Joseph Roty Griotte-Chambertin - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Griotte-Chambertin Grand Cru (5/31/2016)
    TN: Trump BdB, Chardonnay, Piedmont and Burgundy (The Grill Room at Rosewood DC): Youthful nose displaying intense black fruits, blackberry, black cherry, fine dust, rose, sweet spices and earth. Excellent concentration, nicely layered fruits, bright acidity, a hint of tannins and long precise finish. The fruit is purer than the 95 as there is very little roasted fruit. Still need another decade to reach the peak. Nevertheless, quite enjoyable to drink. (95 pts.)
  • 1995 Domaine Joseph Roty Charmes-Chambertin Très Vieilles Vignes - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Charmes-Chambertin Grand Cru (5/31/2016)
    TN: Trump BdB, Chardonnay, Piedmont and Burgundy (The Grill Room at Rosewood DC): Roasted fruit and caramel that I associate with the 95, still very youthful black fruits, coffee, leather and earth. Excellent concentration, nicely layered fruits and long finish. Neither terribly complex nor elegant but showing concentrated opulent sweet fruits. (94 pts.)
  • 2002 Domaine Joseph Roty Mazis-Chambertin - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Mazis-Chambertin Grand Cru (3/25/2015)
    2002 Champagne and Burgundy dinner with Eric (Chez Billy Sud - Washington DC): Expressive nose of fresh blackberries, raspberry jelly, cappuccino, a strong note of wild flowers, mineral and earth. Exceptional concentration, quite dense, opulent dark fruits, bright acidity and noticeable tannins. Some tasters thought the palate was a bit disjointed. For my palate, very impressive and on the right track as it displayed ample fruits and noticeable structure via bright acidity and strong presence of tannins. The nose is already very hedonistic but the palate could come together more with another five to ten years of cellaring. Lovely wine that I highly recommend. (96 pts.)
  • 2009 Domaine Joseph Roty Mazis-Chambertin - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Mazis-Chambertin Grand Cru (3/30/2014)
    2013 editon of En Primeur - Kelly and Kevin’s excellent adventure; 3/26/2014-4/1/2014 (Mostly in Bordeaux and a night in Paris): Pop and pour, this was incredibly tasted. Black fruits, blackberries, blackberry jelly, sweet spices, coffee, milk, mineral and earth. Excellent concentration, silky palate, nicely integrated tannins and lovely long finish. This clearly displays the domaine style, quite intense yet very precise and pure, sort of in the middle ground between the style of C Dugat and Rousseau. Really hedonistic nightcap. (97 pts.)
  • 2001 Domaine Joseph Roty Charmes-Chambertin Très Vieilles Vignes - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Charmes-Chambertin Grand Cru (10/9/2011)
    Mike Grammer Comes to Town - KS version (Dino, Washington DC): Beautiful expressive nose, raspberry preserves, ripe red and black fruits, coffee, dry herbs and earth. The lightest yet the most expressive showing of the flight. Some Cote Rotie like characters which is very similar to the Bachelet. (94 pts.)

Posted from CellarTracker

From my ‘Diary’ in May last year:

The next two needed to be enjoyed together – my two adieu bottles to Charles Rousseau and Philippe Roty:

2000 Armand Rousseau, Chambertin + 2000 Joseph Roty, Griottes-Chambertin – The Rousseau is fresh and direct, lots of energy and complexity here – can it really be a 2000? Move to the Roty and you have more weight, more vestiges of oak, plenty of complexity and weight of texture – it seems more concentrated. Move back to the Rousseau and it’s ‘wow’ fresh and complex – clearly better – move back to the Roty and it’s bigger and more impressive – clearly better! What a fabulous, pairing to compare and contrast – each sip from the other is better than the last – in a different way. Probably the Rousseau is better for the future, but today, this comparison was the quintessence of burgundy brilliance…

posts.name: Michael Lewis
posts.cooked:
Martin Steinley:

Served to me blind, I am not sure that I would guess Burgundy.

A few years ago a friend and mentor served me some Roty from the mid-1990s blind (It may have been the Mazis-Chambertin) and I did in fact guess that it was Syrah from the Northern Rhone. Not that I am typically a great blind taster, but usually I can at least guess the region of Burgundy correctly when served a grand cru Burgundy. Not so in this case. Of course, it did not help that he served it to me in a Bordeaux glass, and then as his “hint” (after I already guessed Northern Rhone) picked up my glass and poured it into a clean Burgundy glass and handed it back to me.

posts.name: Jürgen Steinke
posts.cooked:
Kevin,

true but I am pretty happy that Roty´s wines are flying under the radar. The last thing we need is another Rousseau, Roumier and DRC. The more people dislike Roty the better. Prices will remain at least rather reasonable then.
